coredata.dc:title: An efficient designing of nitrogen decorated carbon quantum dots as Fe fluorescent probe: On account of binary carbon source system
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Huilin
Huang, Qizhang
Li, Qiaoguang
Wu, Lianying
Liao, Haiyan
Hasan, Murtaza
Gong, Sheng
Zhou, Xinhua
coredata.dc:description: Accurate detection of Fe3+ is an urgent need due rapid increase of hemochromatosis, methemoglobinemia, mental decline and physical weakness throughout the world. Here, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) having blue fluorescence were prepared by facile one-spot hydrothermal method by using binary carbon source. This innovative primed hybrid NCQDs were well characterized by using FT-IR, XPS, and EDS and SEM for purpose of chemical composition and surface functionality. The morphology of NCQDs were confirmed their spherical shape and an average diameter was 4.3 nm by TEM measurement. Surface coating for functional group and nitrogen doped was monitored by the FTIR, XPS and EDX analysis. The photoluminescence measurements showed that NCQDs were non-wavelength dependent with a quantum yield of 73.89%. The NCQDs showed strong evidence to be a excellent fluorescent sensor for the rapid and quick estimation of Fe3+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. The fluorescence power of NCQDs were slowly declined with higher amount of Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0.01 to 0.5 and 1–33.3 μM. The accurate range of detection limit was very narrow and found to be 5 nM for Fe3+by this method. Furthermore, the function of novel NCQDs for estimation of trace element such as Fe3+ in different sources of water was yielded at very acceptable recoveries (97.30% ∼ 98.04%). The current designed functional probe provided a promising fluorescent range at narrow level for the Fe3+ detection is highly appreciable in the future studies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensitivity
Carbon quantum
Fe fluorescent
Functional probe
Luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of NCQDs on Fe3+ detection
The efficient synthesis of NCQDs and the mechanism analysis
Selectivity and sensitivity of NCQDs on Fe3+ detection

Acknowledgment is made to the Donors of the Guangdong Education Department (Grant No. 2020ZDZXM1040 ) for partial support of this research.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a ∼ b) TEM images and the insert is the size distribution histogram of NCQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) PL emission spectra; (c) Raman spectra; (d) XRD patterns of NCQDs.
(a) Full scan XPS spectra of NCQDs, (b-d) C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s XPS spectra of NCQDs.
(a) The fluorescence stability of NCQDs, (b) The effect of pH of NCQDs.
(a) The synthesis process of NCQDs, (b) Fluorescence photographs of NCQDs, with the various carbon sources were taken under the ultraviolet light (365 nm).

(a) PL emission spectra of NCQDs under different metal ion interference, (b) PL emission spectra of NCQDs under the different concentrations of Fe3+, (c) Standard curve, (d) Fluorescence photographs of NCQD, with the different concentrations of Fe3+ were taken under ultraviolet light.
One-pot preparation of N-rGO and NCQDs. Fe3+ detection uses the NCQDs.
The QY results of CQDs in different carbon sources.
Comparison of different fluorescent sensors for Fe3+ detection.
Detection of Fe3+ in the real sample by NCQDs.

head.ce:title: An efficient designing of nitrogen decorated carbon quantum dots as Fe fluorescent probe: On account of binary carbon source system

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Zhongkai University of Agriculture and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate detection of Fe3+ is an urgent need due rapid increase of hemochromatosis, methemoglobinemia, mental decline and physical weakness throughout the world. Here, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) having blue fluorescence were prepared by facile one-spot hydrothermal method by using binary carbon source. This innovative primed hybrid NCQDs were well characterized by using FT-IR, XPS, and EDS and SEM for purpose of chemical composition and surface functionality. The morphology of NCQDs were confirmed their spherical shape and an average diameter was 4.3 nm by TEM measurement. Surface coating for functional group and nitrogen doped was monitored by the FTIR, XPS and EDX analysis. The photoluminescence measurements showed that NCQDs were non-wavelength dependent with a quantum yield of 73.89%. The NCQDs showed strong evidence to be a excellent fluorescent sensor for the rapid and quick estimation of Fe3+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. The fluorescence power of NCQDs were slowly declined with higher amount of Fe3+ concentration ranging from 0.01 to 0.5 and 1–33.3 μM. The accurate range of detection limit was very narrow and found to be 5 nM for Fe3+by this method. Furthermore, the function of novel NCQDs for estimation of trace element such as Fe3+ in different sources of water was yielded at very acceptable recoveries (97.30% ∼ 98.04%). The current designed functional probe provided a promising fluorescent range at narrow level for the Fe3+ detection is highly appreciable in the future studies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon oxide is a fundamental trace element for humans and also the most ample element inside the human body that participates in various metabolic pathways including homeostasis, cellular trafficking, and enzyme catalysis [1]. There are many traces element with deficient in amount while some nutrient are found to be in excess form such as Fe3+ causes serious illness like methemoglobinemia, mental decline and physical weakness in the human body [2,3] . The optimum or threshold value for iron is 0.3 ppm that is ∼5.4 μM for proper metabolism and body functioning. As excessive intake of Fe3+ can lead to severe poisoning, whereas limited intake causes anemia that causes severe disorders [4]. Thereby constant monitoring of Fe3+ needs to be addressed for maintain vital body function and human health [5].

Conventionally Fe3+ detection was determined using atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [6], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES) [7], and electrochemical analysis [8]. However, current detection techniques still had many disadvantages, such as, time consuming process, lengthy sampling methodology and used of highly sophisticated instrumentations [9]. Thus, attain reasonable, less expensive, convenient and rapid process for determining Fe3+ with high significance. Later a more advanced method known as fluorometric method was developed, this method is considered as high selectivity, sensitivity, gives rapid analysis, and is quite convenient [10]. Nanotechnology has revolutionized the world for detecting diseases [11], tracing metal analysis [12], improvement is electronics [13], industrial wastes degradation [14], agricultural wastes removal and many other biological [15], chemical and physical analysis [16–18]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has shown potential application value in ion detection due to novel, diverse and their unique appreciable and exceptional fluorescence properties [19].

The novel and unique way of producing CQDs commonly found to be “top-down” method and the “bottom-up” method. The “top-down” method suggests to separate with high intensity of CQDs having extremely small sizes nanoparticle with wide range of distribution based on carbon materials by chemical or physical methods [20]. The “bottom-up” procedure totally recommended for the tailoring of unique CQDs by carbonization and different form of polymerization with addition of continuously small molecules with the support of chemical reactions [21]. Further, the preparation methods are subdivided into hydrothermal synthesis [22], microwave synthesis [23] and chemical oxidation synthesis [24] etc. Hydrothermal method of nanoparticles synthesis is very useful, suitable and very economical compared to mild reaction condition over other methods [25]. Moreover, heteroatom doping is done for enhancing the quantum yield of CQDs [26]. As an example of nitrogen, Ayala et al. pointed out that N doping towards very sensitive and delicate shift of the Fermi level. This is one of the most effective methods of enhancing the luminescence intensity of CQDs [27]. And nitrogen can further promote synchronization of all the molecules as a functional group on the NCQDs surface structure and Fe3+ contents in the biological samples [28,29].

In another reports, Zhang et al. synthesized the nitrogen and boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B1N2CQDs) via a two-step hydrothermal method with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 15.4% [30]. The main origion of B1N2CQDs was citric acid and the quantification limit of Fe3+ (LOD) was found 80 nM. In comparison with the two-step method, the one-step method was more rapid and simpler. Later on, Latief et al. has also revised it and found that gelatin as a active ingredient for the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of NCQDs, the QY was showing about 22.7% and the LOD was about 200 nM [31]. Additionally, NCQDs were also synthesized from other sources such as biomass tar and rice residue, where the QY values were 23.48% and 26.10%, respectively [32,33]. The above-mentioned CQDs which were synthesized through the hydrothermal method are in a single carbon source system. There is still a large improvement in QY. Preserving and enriching the surface-specific chemical groups of CQDs was an important way to enhance the PL [34].

Furthermore, another researcher, Lai et al. prepared a hybrid probe PNDs with carbon as elemental part in different biological materials such as source glucose and glycine which showed a highly remarkable fluorescence as compared to isolated and without biological materials carbon by one-pot hydrothermal treatment [35]. It was believed that continue chemical reactions which move to formed very complex and unique polymer mixtures. These novel and unique polymer mixtures have confirmed presence of rich and active functional groups that would be able to isolate and diagnosis all kinds of defects present on the surface, maybe acts as energy excitation traps mechanism and highlighting to the different fluorescence properties. In another Reports tartaric acid and wheat bran as binary carbon sources to synthesize G-CQDs which QY was about 46% [19].

In this work, we synthesized NCQDs from a binary carbon source by one-pot hydrothermal treatment. In this system, graphene oxide (top-down) and sodium citrate (bottom-up) were used as binary carbon sources, and urea was used as a nitrogen source. Meanwhile, sodium citrate and urea were also used as reducing agents and dopants of graphene oxide. The doping of N2 atoms enhances the surface weaknesses, allowing the re-excitation of excitons trapped by the surface imperfections. In addition, the binary carbon sources system which exhibits a stronger fluorescence than the single source allows a variety of surface functional group states and increased fluorescence properties. The prepared NCQDs fluorescent sensor system shows high permanence, high discrimination, high compassion, and fast response for Fe3+ detection. The NCQDs as fluorescent probe exhibited a linear range within 0.01–0.5 and 1–33.3 μM and a detection limit of 5 nM. The high retrieval in sharp standard samples shows the prospective of the recommended NCQDs for the revealing of Fe3+ in real complex samples.

The fabrication of graphene oxide (GO) was carried out by the already reported method mention in the literature with slight changes [36]. Sodium citrate and quinine sulfate and other chemicals were purchased from commercial company Aladdin Ltd. Instead of this, Urea was also purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. The cation solutions of Ba2+, Na+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ in a concentration of 100 mM were devised from chloride, sulfate, and nitrate. All the chemical and reagents were according to the standard form and were used directly without any further purification.

For the successful preparation and fabrication of nanostructure and characterization, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR, PerkinElmer Spectrum 100), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300), Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos, AXIS Ultra DLD), UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 300 Scan), Fluorescent Spectrometer (PerkinElmer, LS-45).

Initially, 5 mL of GO (10 mg/mL), 1 g of urea, and 1 g of sodium citrate were mixed in 30 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 10 min. Later on, the mixed solutions was poured into a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and reacted at a temperature of 180 ℃ for 10 h. Later, all the mixtures were cold down at room temperature and then the final products were attained by filtration and dialysis for 12 h (cutoff Mn: 500 Da). The cyan-colored NCQDs powder was obtained from the filtrate via freeze-dried.

The QY of NCQDs were measured by an established procedure using the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as a standard solution (QYs=54%) [37]. The QY of NCQDs is calculated by the formula as 73.89%. The calculation equation of QY was as follow:
(1)
QY
c
=
QY
s
(
Y
c
A
c
) (
A
s
Y
s
) (
η
c
2
η
s
2
) = 54 % (
52712
0.014
) (
0.017
46756
) (
1.3327
2
1.3330
2
) = 73.89 %
Where A is the UV absorbance, Y is the integrated area of the PL, and η is the refractive index. The subscript "c” and "s” refers to NCQDs and Quinine sulfate, respectively.

To investigate the fluorescence stability of NCQDs, the NCQDs solution (0.0027 mg/mL) was irradiated under the xenon lamp and sampled every 5 min, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded at different light times. The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs were measured under different pH conditions to investigate the effect of pH on NCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of NCQDs on Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The detection method was followed according to the previously reported literature [38]. In the experiment of specific selectivity for the detection of metals ions included Ba2+, Ni2+, K+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cu2+, and Fe3+ were used, and each concentration was 100 mM in NCQDs solution (0.0027 mg/mL). All the fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded at a wavelength of 440 nm and excited at 340 nm after reaction for 15 min at room temperature, respectively. For sensitivity detection, the Fe3+ solution at different concentrations was added (the concentration range was 0.01 ∼ 33.30 μM) into NCQDs solution (0.0027 mg/mL). Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at a wavelength of 440 nm and excited at 340 nm after reaction for 5 min at room temperature, respectively [39].

To verify the reliability and accuracy of the planned chemosensing system, spiking tests were carried out on Pearl River water without any treatment. The solution of Fe3+ with various concentrations were added (1 μM, 10 μM, 30 μM) into the NCQDs solution. After shaking, the fluorescence intensity of the Fe3+ solution was determined at the optimal excitation wavelength, respectively. The spiked recoveries were calculated by comparing the concentration of Fe3+ calculated from the standard curve with the concentration of Fe3+ actually added.

The One-pot preparation of N-rGO and NCQDs and Fe3+ ions were detected by using NCQDs as shown in Scheme 1. The TEM morphology of the NCQDs were shown in ( Fig. 1 a), and it can be observed that the NQCDs were spherical in shape with an average particle size of approximately 4.3 nm. The homogeneous monodisperse state of NCQDs indicates good water solubility and good dispersion in water. Further, the HRTEM of the NCQDs were shown in (Fig. 1 b). Most of the NCQDs particles showed no obvious lattice stripes, while a few particles showed obvious lattice stripes with the lattice spacing of 0.216 nm.

The well characterization was performed by different spectroscopic techniques such as FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to ensure the presence of different chemical functional compound with different functionality on the upper surface of the NCQDs ( Fig. 2 a). The peak at 3409 cm−1 shows a wide absorption band which was approved for O-H/N-H stretching vibration peaks of the NCQDs. Similarly, the wide absorption band indicated the rich oxygen and nitrogen-containing groups on NCQDs’ surface. The peak at 1606 cm−1 showed the stretching vibration peak of CO, which suggested the carboxylic acid groups (-COONa) of NCQDs. Whereas, the characteristic peak of-CH2-/C-N appeared at 1401 cm−1 which confirmed the graphene carbon skeleton and the nitrogen doping on the NCQDs. The diversity of surface functional groups enhances the fluorescence properties of NCQDs [40], residing properties of biological and chemical functional group present on the surface’s nanomaterials and their applications as previously reported by researchers [41–43].

The PL emission chromatographic spectra of NCQDs at various wavelengths were determined to explore their fluorescence properties. There were various profiles with excitation spectra attained from self-prepared NCQDs at various excitation spectral profiles (290–380 nm) shown in Fig. 2 b which clearly shown that a sharper and more intense peak was observed at 340 nm. This spectrum did not show the typical excitation wavelength dependence and its peak position remains constant. Current results showed the better emission spectra as compared to previously work [44,45].

The extent of defects and graphitization of NCQDs were investigated by Raman and XRD spectra (Fig. 2 c,d). The D peak in the Raman spectrum is represent structural defects and amorphous carbon, while the G peak is represent ordered sp2 hybridized carbon. (Fig. 2 c) shows that NCQDs have distinct D and G peaks, and the intensity of the D peak is significantly higher than the G peak. It indicates that NCQDs have a high degree of defects and a locally irregular graphene structure. Further, the XRD spectrum of NCQDs shows (Fig. 2 d). a distinct broad peak at 21.2° that is slightly closer to the (002) peak indicating that the crystalline nature of NCQDs is poor, possibly because NCQDs contain many oxygen-containing functional groups. Combining the results of TEM, Raman, and XRD, the final structure of the NCQDs is considered to be an amorphous graphite-like structure.

To know the chemical information of materials and surface state of the NCQDs, we have performed XPS analysis. The complete profile was obtained by scanning XPS spectrum NCQDs and was presented as 284 eV, 399 eV, and 531 eV, which showed that carbon has (C 1 s), nitrogen (N 1 s), and oxygen (O 1 s) respectively ( Fig. 3 a). However, the high-resolution chromatographic spectrum of C 1 s was showing in (Fig. 3 b) with clear separated into four peaks at 284.6, 286.3, 288.0, and 289.4 eV, which were authentic proof of the four types of carbon bonds (C–C, CC, C-N, and C-O, respectively). The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s illustrated in (Fig. 3 c) showed three chromatographic peaks at 530.9 eV, 531.8 eV, and 535.5 eV that were assigned to CO, C-OH, and C-O-C respectively. The given results regarding the high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s presented in (Fig. 3 d) which were undoubtedly presenting two peaks at 399.5 and 400.4 eV that were strongly linked with N in a graphite-like structure (N-C3) and N-H bond of -NH2 groups, respectively. Therefore, from the above presented evidences that N was positively doped into the complex and unique framework of NCQDs. These results also confirmed the occurrence of hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl functional groups on NCQDs upper surface which was in promise with the results achieved from FT-IR [18,46,47]. The SEM image showed two distinct morphologies (Fig SI). That indicated that NCQDs were efficiently synthesized by the binary source system. Sodium citrate was converted to NCQDs by a “bottom-up” method. Fragment GO was converted to NCQDs by a “top-down” approach in a high-temperature and high-pressure environment of the reaction kettle. Furthermore, the composition of NCQDs were analyzed by the mapping of their element distribution. The elemental mapping assured the existence of C, O, N, and Na on the upper surface as illustrated in (Fig SI d-g), respectively. The NCQDs synthesized from binary carbon source systems were complex mixtures. Thus, there is a difference in the elemental distribution on the NCQDs surface. The atomic percentages histogram of NCQDs can be analyzed with larger O and N percentages (Fig SI h). This shows that the dual carbon source system can bring rich and diverse functional groups [48].

Fig. 4a reveals that the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs does not change significantly with increasing irradiation time. It is possible that NCQDs can compensate for it surface defects through photooxidation and surface carboxyl groups. The excellent photostability of NCQDs indicates it suitability as fluorescent probes for the long-term detection of heavy metals.

The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs was measured under different pH conditions. The results showed that NCQDs had a significant pH dependence. The fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased, and the fluorescence intensity was maximum at pH = 6. The main reason may be attributed to the surface of NCQDs which is rich in acidic functional groups such as carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups. In a strongly alkaline environment, the acidic functional groups bind to OH-, which reduces the surface defects of NCQDs and affects the fluorescence intensity. In a strongly acidic environment, NCQDs were easily prone to protonation which also leads to fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The efficient synthesis of NCQDs and the mechanism analysis
ce:section.ce:para: A key indicator of the fluorescence performance of CQDs is the QY [49]. The results of QY were presented clearly in Table 1 . The N with composition of CQDs shown in the table was determined by elemental distribution mapping. In the unary carbon source system, unary-SC used sodium citrate as the carbon source and QY was found about 13.58%. Whereas unary-GO used GO as the carbon source, and no CQDs were produced. The QY of Unary-N-SC (36.98%) and Unary-N-GO (8.50%) which used urea as nitrogen dopant was much higher than that of Unary-SC and Unary-GO, confirming that N-doping could enhance the fluorescence intensity and the previous reports had illustrated that the inclusion in the hybrid materials and the role of N an essential for efficient tailoring the absorption and emission behaviors of CQDs due to very high surface defects being generated after N doping [50,51]. In the binary carbon source system, the QY of Binary-N (73.89%) was higher than that of the undoped nitrogen carbon source system (21.03%). Owing to the N-doping, the QY of Binary-N was 3.5 times higher than Binary, suggesting that the binary carbon source system is an efficient method for the preparation of NCQDs.

A possible mechanism of high QY for NCQDs illustrated in Fig. 5a. In this binary carbon source system, high level of fluorescence intensity was achieved by the NCQDs from the crossbreed carbon source of GO and sodium citrate which can be certified to two main factors: (1) sodium citrate played two important roles, one was carbon source for NCQDs and the other was the reducing agent of GO. Some sodium citrate molecule enters the gap and spread the layer of GO. The presence of carboxyl groups created electrostatic repulsion with the carboxyl groups which is present on the rich surface of graphene oxide [17,52]. Furthermore, increasing the high-temperature and high-pressure environment of the reaction kettle, some of the GO with thin lamellae were easily further exfoliated and broken, and eventually converted into NCQDs [40,47,53,54]. Some sodium citrate was converted to NQCDs by a “bottom-up” method. (2) Preserving and enriching the surface-specific chemical groups of CQDs performances an imperative role in enhancing the PL [24,55].

Binary carbon source systems may have many chemical reactions and diversity in their end products which were very complex polymer blends [56–58]. The obtained results of XPS and FTIR clearly demonstrated that the hybrid polymer mixtures have very rich functional groups, such as COOH, OH, and NH2. In fact, the mechanism is currently not clear known yet and it is an urgent need to investigate it further in-depth level. From Fig. 5b, it has been a visual comparison with samples from different carbon sources showed that NCQDs had the best fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of NCQDs on Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, there were many PL fluorescence sensors were present for accurate detection at very delicate and selective candidate of metal ions established on oxygen as active working groups present on the upper superficial of CQDs which has ability to make strong bond with metal ions to formed very rich complexed materials with multiple function. However, it has also been observed that Fluorescence quenching intensity and the level of NCQDs after mixing with various metal ions (including Ba2+, Na+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+, the concentration of 100 μM) presented in Fig. 6a. It was further noticed that these metals ions or additive in the complex in isolated form didn’t showing no any apparent quenching fluorescence intensity in comparison with the blank. However, we have found that the level of fluorescence strength of the NCQDs ensued in presence of Fe3+ within 15 min was continuously decreasing, which implies that the addition of Fe3+ could stimulate the appeasing of fluorescence emission of NCQDs in an aqueous solution. Thus, from the above investigation and results indicates that NCQDs can be used as a strong candidate for detecting Fe3+. According to the already reported data about the Fe3+ ions have good results which had a strong attachment affinity and quick chelating kinetic with the carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups of NCQDs [23]. Existence of many functional groups on the top of NCQDs caused a narrower band gap of the NCQDs. The NCQDs with narrower band gap may make the LUMO of NCQDs earlier to the redox potential of Fe3+. Therefore, the electrons of NCQDs may move from the LUMO levels of NCQDs to Fe3+ more easily, instead of transferring to HUMO levels of NCQDs.1 It encourages the alteration in the electronic structure of NCQDs that imitate the dispersal of excitons. This transformation causes the non-radiative recombination instead of the innovative radiation recombination, which leads to NCQDs fluorescence quenching. While CQDs deficiency functional groups, the range of wide band gap may enable it to correspondence with redox potentials of many metal ions, which causes CQDs to answer to numerous metal ions and leads to insignificant selectivity.

The NCQDs were also used in many applications in recognition of contaminates in the water, food and other stuffs which gives a sensing platform for the detection of Fe3+ ions and other metallic ions and the accurate and fast revealing capability of the analytical system for Fe3+ was evaluated [59]. In the current work, it was also investigated that the process of Fe3+ concentration-dependent quenching of the fluorescence of NCQDs (Fig. 6b) totally depend on concentration. The fluorescence strength can be reflected quenched entirely when the concentration of Fe3+ was supplementary up to 100 μM. The relative PL intensity of the NQCDs was found linear to the Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0.01–0.5 μM and 1–33.3 μM with a linear equation: y = −81.781x + 823.89 (R2 =0.9905) and y = −5.204x + 721.26 (R2 =0.9914) shown in Fig. 5c, where the LOD was calculated 5 nM. From Fig. 5d, the comprehensive comparison was showing clear difference with previous reported known concentration and current used concentration samples indicated that Fe3+ induced a prodigious magnitude of quenching in fluorescence. And the quenching efficiency was over 90%, indicating good sensitivity to Fe3+.

Table 2 shows the comparison of the detection limit of the NCQDs to Fe3+ with other detection methods. Although the NCQDs probe in current work bestows a equivalent linear range and LOD, NCQDs have high quantum yields. High QY means that the consumption rate of materials can be further enhanced which is in line with the concept of green chemistry and can lessen costs and increase benefits [60].

From previous work review reports comparison, it can be seen that present work has shown better and remarkable specificity collective with high understanding and quick response of NCQDs to Fe3+ suggested that this method give assurance of authentic results which obtained from directly applied for detecting Fe3+ in real samples. To validate the reliability and authenticity of planned new and novel chemo sensing system for estimation and determination, the Pearl River water without any treatment was tested. Confounded samples were expended to implement recovery tests and has shown the trustworthiness, sensitivity and accuracy of the method. Different amount of Fe3+ was added to the samples and Fe3+ concentration was strongminded in each sample at ideal conditions. After evaluating the Fe3+ gist of different samples the repossessions were designed. Despite the interference from several minerals and organics presented in river water, this sensing system can be highly selective toward the practical Fe3+ detection in real samples. These results clearly demonstrated that the NCQDs probe was prospective to be proficient of realistically useful Fe3+ recognition upon further improvement. Table 3.

Conclusively, NCQDs which were non-wavelength dependent with greatest yield of 73.89% were efficiently fabricated by a binary carbon source system. Where the N-doping led to high surface defects which gave the NCDQs an extremely high PL. And the binary carbon source system makes the rich surface of NCQDs rich in different practical groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, and pyridine nitrogen. The fluorescence satisfying mechanism is due to very centers formed between NCQDs’ surface functional groups and Fe3+. As a novel fluorescence probe, the NCQDs have high selectivity and sensitivity, and the detection limit was found to be 5 nM in the linear range from 0.01 to 0.5 and 1–33.3 μM. Furthermore, the practicability of the planned method has been demonstrated by the purpose of Fe3+ in actual samples with acceptable results.

Sheng Gong, Murtaza Hasan, and Haiyan Liao designed the study. Huilin Gao, Haiyan Liao, Lianying Wu, Qiaoguang Li, and Qizhang Huang prepared the samples and conducted the tests/analyses. Sheng Gong, Huilin Gao and Haiyan Liao wrote the manuscript. Murtaza Hasan, and Xinhua Zhou revised it. All authors gave final approval for publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was partially supported by Graduate Science and Technology Innovation Fund of Zhongkai University of Agriculture and Engineering (KA220160262) and by Guangdong Science and Technology Department Grant No. 2018A050506081. Acknowledgment is made to the Donors of the Guangdong Education Department (Grant No. 2020ZDZXM1040) for partial support of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.mtcomm.2023.105707.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
